description_long: This object is a solid metal crowbar, commonly used as a prying tool in urban environments such as construction sites, garages, or for emergency access. It features a long, straight shaft with a flattened, wedge-shaped end on one side and a curved, forked claw on the other, designed for pulling nails or prying apart materials. The entire tool is made from a single piece of metal, likely steel, giving it a uniform, dark finish with a slightly matte appearance. The shaft is round and tapers slightly towards both ends, providing leverage and strength. The forked end is split into two prongs, allowing it to grip and extract embedded objects, while the opposite end is beveled for wedging into tight spaces. There are no visible grips, insulation, or branding on the surface, and the tool's design is utilitarian and robust.
description: A solid metal crowbar with a straight shaft, a beveled wedge end, and a curved, forked claw for prying and pulling nails. The finish is dark and matte, with no visible grips or branding.
description_view_0: Side view showing the full length of the crowbar, highlighting the straight shaft and the distinct curved, forked claw at one end.
description_view_1: Angled view emphasizing the curvature of the claw and the thickness of the shaft, with the forked end facing forward.
description_view_2: Opposite side view displaying the straight shaft and the wedge-shaped end, with the forked claw curving away.
description_view_3: Angled view from the other side, showing the curvature of the claw and the flatness of the wedge end.
attribute_handle_type: Straight, round metal shaft with no insulation or ergonomic features.
attribute_jaw_design: Curved, forked claw with two prongs for nail pulling; opposite end is a flat, beveled wedge for prying.
attribute_pivot_mechanism: None; the tool is a single solid piece with no moving parts or pivot.
attribute_grip_pattern: Smooth metal surface with no added grip pattern or texture.
